messages.content: You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Describe the research methodology, technical approach, risk mitigation strategies, and TRL progression.
Write the Methodology and approach section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

1.3 METHODOLOGY RECLAIM aspires to develop a compact, portable, automated and intelligent material recovery facility, our prMRF (Fig.1.2), which increases the scope of material recovery by covering areas with locally high waste treatment needs and enabling material recovery from waste streams that are not adequately treated by existing municipal systems. Our project will deliver a technologically and scientifically sound solution and demonstrate its application in difficult real environments with high demands in terms of waste treatment and material recovery (see Box 2 summarizing project scenarios). Complementary to the above, RECLAIM develops a new gaming infrastructure that increases social sensitivity on recycling and promotes the active participation of citizens in the research activities of our project. Overall, the proposed work is structured in three phases (Figure 1.4) that foresee implementation and release of three incrementally enhanced versions of the targeted technology. The first is the Machinery Integration phase [M1-M9] that will capitalize on available technology and the extensive expertise of our consortium in applied waste management solutions, to quickly implement the infrastructure and assemble the mechanical equipment that is necessary for the operation of our prMRF. This phase will additionally focus on the early integration of existing hardware and software robotic technology in order to promptly reveal/mitigate conflicts in the waste processing chain. Moreover, it will enable data collection to accelerate the training of the necessary AI modules and facilitate the design of environmental games targeting recycling and particular material recovery. This rapidly created complete prMRF prototype will provide the basis for the second Lean Research and Development27 phase28 [M10-M30] that aims at implementing, testing in the field and continuously improving solutions for the AI-ILC and RoReWo-Team modules considering the local characteristics of each deployment site. A lean implementation that follows a test-fast/improve-fast approach for the immediate reveal and relief of problems in the field, is particularly appropriate for creating a prMRF that is functional and highly productive in real conditions. Moreover, the second phase will consider the use of real world data for RDG implementation to facilitate data collection by citizens. Figure 1.4. Schematic representation of the RECLAIM development plan. The last, Wrap-Up and Consolidation phase [M31-M36] focuses on the continuous and uninterrupted operation of the deployed prMRF with small-scale optimizations on the combined operation of the mechanical and computing modules. This is the main result of our project that will be presented to the public complemented by the RDG environmental games to increase public sensitivity on recycling and support prMRF sustainability. The final prMRF is considered as the Minimum Viable Product (MVP) of our project that will be demonstrated to selected audiences such as the scientific community, stakeholders interested in waste management and potential investors for considering its commercial exploitation. Further to our prMRF, the exploitation and potential commercialization of other project assets (RoReWos, AI-ILC, RDG) will be actively considered in the third phase of our project. The following sections summarize the work to be done in the three development phases of RECLAIM, focusing separately on the four innovation pillars of our project. 1.3.1 Machinery Integration The first phase focuses on the direct exploitation of the technology available to us, partners FORTH, KUL, IRIS, RBNS and the waste management/material recovery expertise available to HRRC, ION, AIMPLAS to develop an early version of the targeted prMRF, which will provide a solid basis for experimentation, thereby facilitating successful execution of our overall project work plan. 27 Kalinowski, M. et al. (2020) Lean R&D: An Agile Research and Development Approach for Digital Transformation. Int. Conf. Product-Focused Software Process Improvement 28 Due to the significant expertise of our consortium on all background technologies, a “lean” development approach is followed in RECLAIM to emphasize focused, field-specific customizations. HORIZON-CL4-2021-DIGITAL-EMERGING-01-09: AI, data and Robotics for the Green Deal (IA) [RECLAIM] Page 12 of 45 PIL-1: Advanced AI for material identification, localization and categorization (AI-ILC) The first phase of our project will consider the purchase/installation of visual monitoring equipment and the use of available solutions contributed by project partners to enable the collection of recyclable material imaging data, using RGB and hyperspectral cameras. The data collected will be used to immediately start the training of Neural Networks for the AI-ILC modules, even in the first phase of our project. Data collection will be facilitated by the use of a quick annotation tool developed by RBNS, which will allow us to select the entire pixel area of the appearing object with a single click on an image29. The tool will be significantly improved in RECLAIM in terms of usability, accuracy and the ability to process hyperspectral data. PIL-2: New, modular, low-cost, high performance, multi-robot/multi-gripper recyclable recovery During the first months of our project, a targeted design and implementation of the RoReWo unit will be carried out. The RoReWo assumes a simplified architecture to achieve a significant reduction in hardware costs by trading off small reductions in robot efficiency and productivity. The RoReWo consists of a single axis closed loop motion control Cartesian robot that can be installed above any size conveyor belt transporting the waste, with its motion direction perpendicular to the movement of the belt. This allows the robot carriage to run along the entire width of the belt. A low-cost piston is mounted on the moving carriage of the robot, which moves vertically in relation to the conveyor belt (i.e. up/down), with the use of compressed air. The piston moves towards the belt in order to reach and grab a recyclable object and then away from the belt to pick and transfer the object to the material collection bins installed on the left and right side of the RoReWo. Attached to the piston is one of the different available end-effectors that grabs and holds the objects when transported to the collection bin. The synchronization of the moving robot carriage, the piston and the grabber is achieved using Newtonian mechanisms that combine the prediction of the location of objects on the belt to position the robot at the right time above the object to be sorted, and then timely activate the motion of the piston and the grabber. A particularly low cost RoReWo prototype has been already evaluated in relevant waste treatment conditions30. The RoReWo described above is of particularly low cost (approx. 4K €) and achieves ~ 45 picks per minute, when the commonly used Delta robots (approx. cost 25K €) achieve ~60 picks per minute. As a result, by using a 4-RoReWo Team working cooperatively for recyclable material recovery, we can triple the performance of the composite system and still have a lower implementation cost in relation to the Delta robot. Moreover, as shown in Figure 1.5, various grippers are already available by RBNS and KUL, which can be used as end-effectors of the different RoReWos in the team. The synthesis of a multi-robot/multi-gripper team is a significant advantage of the proposed modular RECLAIM approach. In contrast to the use of a single high-cost robot that carries only one end-effector, this approach expands the scope of the composite system and increases the utility and performance of the RoReWo Team for material recovery applications. Figure 1.5. A variety of different grippers that can be used in each RoReWo Long diameter suction cup for deformed packages Short diameter suction cup for small plastic films Fingered gripper for mid-volume and heavy objects Pinned gripper for woods and soft heavy objects Magnetic gripper for ferrous metals PIL-3: Portable robotic Material Recovery Facility For the rapid progress of the first phase there is already a cost plan for the Recycling Equipment (R-Eq) that will be installed in our RECLAIM deployments. During the first months of our project, the R-Eq features will be specified in detail, so that the machinery can be selected and purchased. At the same time, prMRF deployment sites will be examined in order to carry out preparatory actions necessary for prMRF operation. Regarding prMRF deployment for material recovery from post-consumer waste, HRRC has already analyzed the population fluctuations of the Greek islands during a full year period and how these changes affect the amount of recyclables that should be recovered by our prMRF. Based on this analysis, it has already determined that the most suitable island for the installation of our prMRF is Kefalonia with a second (alternative) option, the island of Lefkada. 29 Video link: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HALgk1kb6Xk 30 Video link: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ztffZwWAc44 HORIZON-CL4-2021-DIGITAL-EMERGING-01-09: AI, data and Robotics for the Green Deal (IA) [RECLAIM] Page 13 of 45 Both of these islands face a problem in their waste management all year round, with the problem becoming very intense during the summer months. Thus, the double-shift operation of our prMRF during the tourist season and single-shift in the off-season months is expected to cover a significant part of the island's waste management needs. ION is the local authority responsible for the management of waste in both Kefalonia and Lefkada, being particularly interested to not only host our prMRF throughout the project offering the necessary infrastructure for its operation, but also consider the use of additional prMRFs to address the material recovery needs in other Ionian islands. PIL-4
